section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Abstract
paragraphs.text: This article examines the silent death of EU consumer law in the face of digital transformation. It argues that the traditional consumer protection framework—once grounded in the figure of the consumer, the principle of fairness, and the standard of professional diligence—has been eroded by legal fragmentation, regulatory displacement, and the conceptual fading of the consumer as a distinct legal subject. Sector-specific instruments such as the Digital Services Act, Digital Markets Act, and Artificial Intelligence Act have sidelined horizontal protections and reframed individuals as “users” or “natural persons,” weakening the normative and institutional coherence of consumer law. In response, the paper calls for a resilient revival through a Digital Fairness Act: a dedicated horizontal instrument addressing the structural and behavioural asymmetries of digital markets. Anchored in the concept of digital professional diligence, the proposed Digital Fairness Act would restore fairness and enforceability by targeting manipulative design, algorithmic opacity, and autonomy-distorting commercial practices. The article offers a doctrinal blueprint for such reform, arguing that only a systemic recalibration can meaningfully reinvigorate consumer law in the algorithmic age.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Introduction: Unfairness First
paragraphs.text: The term “digital fairness” is gaining traction in EU political and academic discourse. Yet, its contours remain ambiguous, functioning more as a rhetorical device than a coherent legal standard. In his 2024 mission letter, Commissioner Michael McGrath was explicitly tasked with giving legal shape to this notion by developing a Digital Fairness Act (“DFA”). While the DFA does not currently exist as a formal legislative proposal, the political mandate—combined with the European Commission’s recent Fitness Check on EU

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Introduction: Unfairness First
paragraphs.text: consumer law—signals institutional readiness for reform. This paper responds directly to that moment by offering a normative blueprint for what such an instrument could entail. It proposes a renewed legal foundation for fairness in the digital economy grounded in consumer protection principles, engaging with the legal design of the DFA as a much-needed regulatory reset. Particular attention is paid to treating unfair commercial practices, where digital manipulation, opacity, and power asymmetries remain insufficiently addressed by the current EU consumer acquis.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Introduction: Unfairness First
paragraphs.text: The decision to narrow the scope of the DFA to commercial practices is not merely a matter of legislative pragmatism. It is a matter of conceptual clarity and legal necessity. First, unfair commercial practices remain the most direct and persistent source of consumer harm in digital markets. These harms—from manipulative design patterns to personalized targeting and algorithmic nudging—are addressed only in fragmented and often insufficient ways across the broader consumer law framework. Second, focusing on commercial practices allows the DFA to restore and modernize the core protective function of the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (“UCPD”), which, despite its foundational role, is increasingly outpaced by technological developments. Rather than overextending the DFA into areas already governed by the Unfair Contract Terms Directive (“UCTD”) or the Consumer Rights Directive (“CRD”)—both of which raise distinct conceptual and enforcement challenges (see “The Silent Death or the Dawn of Revival?”)—a targeted approach offers the best chance of legal coherence, enforceability, and political feasibility. Third, this narrowed scope ensures complementarity rather than redundancy with other legal instruments. Many systemic problems affecting consumers today—such as algorithmic discrimination or platform lock-in—could be more effectively tackled through a modernized fairness standard applicable to all digital B2C commercial practices. Attempting to fold broader issues into the DFA would not only risk regulatory overlap but also dilute the normative force of the act. By concentrating on commercial practices, the DFA can serve as a horizontal safety net within the digital single market.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Introduction: Unfairness First
paragraphs.text: This paper takes the position that such a safety net is urgently needed. While the rhetoric of digital fairness gains ground, the legal foundations of fairness in EU consumer law have eroded. The DFA offers an opportunity not only to restore those foundations but to update them by introducing a tailored standard—digital professional diligence—responsive to structural, relational, and informational asymmetries. This concept is introduced here as a central doctrinal innovation and will be elaborated further.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Introduction: Unfairness First
paragraphs.text: The structure of the article is as follows: Section"Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law" explains how EU consumer law is being eroded by fragmented digital legislation and outdated legal frameworks, which fail to address new forms of digital manipulation and vulnerability, ultimately marginalising the consumer as a distinct legal subject in the digital era.; Section "Implications: Fragility of Consumer Protection" explains how the fragmentation of EU consumer law and the rise of digital market regulation have weakened its foundational principles, leading to structural legal gaps, economic distortions, and systemic social harms; Section "Expectations: A Resilient Revival of Consumer Law" argues that rather than relying on fragmented reforms, the EU must adopt a dedicated DFA—a comprehensive, forward-looking regulation rooted in digital professional diligence and designed to restore coherence, autonomy, and fairness in consumer protection across evolving digital markets and Section "Conclusions: Beyond the Horizon" argues that future consumer protection in digital markets requires a distinct, adaptive legal framework capable of evolving with technological change, rather than relying on static, omnibus-style legislation that risks becoming obsolete upon implementation.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
A Holey Patchwork of Approaches
paragraphs.text: The EU lawmaker has little choice but to adopt digital policy legislation—a stark admission of a regime compelled to innovate in the face of dynamic technological change. Digital markets, dominated by powerful online platforms, driven by sophisticated algorithms, and fuelled by relentless data exploitation, present challenges that EU consumer law struggles to address in its traditional form. Instruments such as the Digital Services Act (“DSA”) and the Digital Markets Act (“DMA”) have been introduced to regulate online platform responsibilities and to foster a level playing field. At the same time, the Artificial Intelligence Act (“AIA”) seeks to manage the risks associated with AI systems and their impacts on natural persons. Although these legislative measures are not designed as core consumer law, they ostensibly carry the mantle of safeguarding consumer rights in the digital age. This trend towards sector-specific regulation reflects a broader fragmentation, whereby no single legislative instrument assumes full responsibility for the consumer as a legal subject. This disaggregation results in normative dilution and regulatory overlap.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
A Holey Patchwork of Approaches
paragraphs.text: However, their sector-specific focus casts a long shadow over the foundational principles of EU consumer law. By addressing distinct aspects of the digital ecosystem, these laws marginalize consumers' vulnerabilities. The AIA, for instance, emphasizes transparency and accountability in AI systems; however, it fails to integrate these objectives with broader consumer protection imperatives such as fairness. This disjunction creates a legal scenery where digital policy legislation governs isolated dimensions of the digital economy, leaving the comprehensive protection of consumer rights in abeyance. This disjointed regime limits the reach of individual instruments and undermines the possibility of a unified digital fairness framework—one in which consumer protection is treated as a systemic concern rather than a residual benefit. Similarly, while the DMA and the DSA seek to enhance market competition and enforce platform accountability, they often regard consumers as secondary beneficiaries rather than central stakeholders. These instruments risk relegating consumer welfare to a secondary consideration by focusing predominantly on market regulation and structural remedies. They also perpetuate existing regulatory gaps and create new ones, arising from fragmented protective measures in new digital legislation, a lack of laws, or inadequate legislation (Helberger et al. 2021b).

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
A Holey Patchwork of Approaches
paragraphs.text: Nevertheless, a fully cohesive system capable of addressing every infringement of consumer rights in the digital realm is not feasible. Admittedly, a significant source of regulatory gaps is fast-paced technological innovation; new technologies and business models frequently emerge that fall outside the scope of existing legislation. Laws crafted in response to yesterday's issues may be ill-equipped to confront the realities of tomorrow. Conversely, attributing these difficulties solely to the rapid evolution of the digital market would be an oversimplification. Legislative complexities further complicate the situation. Regulators may intentionally leave gaps, favouring a lighter touch in the hope that the market will self-regulate or due to concerns that overregulation might stifle innovation. At times, entrenched interests may resist certain legislative measures.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
A Holey Patchwork of Approaches
paragraphs.text: Furthermore, the quality of legislative drafting must not be overlooked. Imprecise language, ambiguous terminology, and vaguely constructed provisions often result in multiple interpretations, compromising the consistent application of consumer protection standards. A recent example is Article 5 AIA, which prohibits certain practices that, while not explicitly consumer-focused, may arise in B2C contexts. To facilitate its interpretation, the

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
A Holey Patchwork of Approaches
paragraphs.text: European Commission issued 135-page guidelines—a notable effort to clarify a provision meant to prohibit certain practices in clear terms (European Commission, 2025). Yet even with such guidance, various enforcement mechanisms and differing interpretations across EU Member States further exacerbate these inefficiencies, widening the gaps in what should ideally be a coherent framework.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
A Holey Patchwork of Approaches
paragraphs.text: The emergence of new legal issues does not automatically necessitate legislative reform. A well-grounded tradition in EU private law holds that existing general clauses can be reinterpreted to absorb new challenges, particularly in civil law systems that value continuity and legal certainty through judicial development (Twigg-Flesner, 2017). The UCPD's general clause, akin to flexible standards in other legal instruments, such as Article 3(1) UCTD, exemplifies this adaptive potential. Yet, this approach presumes the continued existence of a clearly defined legal subject to protect—the consumer. As digital practices become more opaque, embedded, and systemic, traditional tools begin to fail, and so does the consumer's legal visibility. The subject, around which fairness-based regulation was built, is at risk of conceptual erasure. While interpretive resilience remains essential in civil law systems, it is no longer sufficient in the face of persistent structural opacity and automation. What is needed is not a reinterpretation but a recalibration of consumer law's foundations in light of digital asymmetries.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
A Holey Patchwork of Approaches
paragraphs.text: These developments raise a more fundamental concern: as the regulatory net stretches and frays, the very figure it was designed to protect—the consumer—begins to disappear.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: In the digital age, the once-distinct figure of the consumer—an individual engaging in transactions for non-commercial purposes—is at risk of vanishing. This phenomenon reflects a broader transformation in legislation, where EU digital policy prioritizes general categories such as “users” (e.g. Article 25(3)(b) DSA; Article 71 AIA), “end users” (Article 2 point 20 of the DMA), “citizens” (e.g. Article 45(3) DSA), “recipients” (Article 26(1) DSA), and “natural persons” (e.g. Article 78 AIA). While these terms are inclusive and capture the multifaceted roles that individuals play in digital ecosystems, they dilute the specific and targeted protection measures that have historically underpinned EU consumer law.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: Traditionally, the concept of the consumer was delineated to capture the vulnerabilities and asymmetries present in B2C transactions. Consumer law was founded on the recognition that consumers, due to their position in the market, need a special regime designed to counterbalance the power of commercial entities. In contrast, recasting individuals as “users,” “citizens,” or “recipients” tends to obscure this dimension. The term “user” may suggest a degree of activity. Still, regulatory discourse often downplays the structural imbalances and manipulative design patterns in digital markets—emphasizing participation over protection. Similarly, “citizen” denotes rights and responsibilities within a civic context, shifting the regulatory discourse from market interactions to broader societal concerns. While this may signal an aspirational expansion of protection, it also risks displacing the economic specificity of consumer vulnerability. What emerges is a gradual erosion of the consumer's legal identity.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: A hierarchy thus appears to be emerging: the “citizen” as an engaged rights-holder, the “user” as a functional participant, and the “consumer” as a market actor whose needs are increasingly framed in narrow, transactional terms. Likewise, referring to individuals as “recipients” diminishes the focus on their active vulnerability in transactional relationships,

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: reducing their role to merely receiving goods or services without acknowledging the power imbalances that often prevail. Core characteristics of consumer transactions—such as exposure to unfair commercial practices—risk becoming invisible in regulatory and policy debates.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: This terminological shift carries far-reaching implications for the substance of EU consumer law. As the regulatory agenda shifts towards addressing systemic issues—such as digital platform governance and algorithmic accountability—consumer protection becomes less visible, less urgent, and more fractured. Instruments like the DSA, the DMA, and the AIA are crafted with a structural logic that emphasizes systemic fairness and innovation, as well as consumers' everyday transactional dilemmas.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: The disintegration of the legal framework further exacerbates this problem. By disaggregating consumer protection into a series of sector-specific regulations, policymakers risk losing sight of the holistic mandate, thus signalling a broader deprioritization of consumer interests. The focus now shifts towards various regulatory imperatives that treat consumer protection as one element among many. This shift represents a fundamental departure from the ethos of EU consumer law, which has traditionally centred regulatory efforts around the consumer.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: The fragmentation of consumer laws and the dissolution of the consumer have led to the silent death of EU consumer law—a gradual weakening of a legal regime's ability to fulfil its foundational objectives. It is characterized not by a single legislative or judicial failure but rather by the cumulative effect of reconfigured laws in which consumer rights are relegated to the status of ancillary benefits within broader market regulation. This transformation undermines the relevance of EU consumer law, making it increasingly reactive and disconnected from its original mandate. Once a clearly defined legal subject endowed with specific rights and protection tools, the consumer is increasingly subsumed into a nebulous category that fails to capture the transactional inequities of digital commerce.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: While this paper describes the vanishing consumer as a structural and conceptual trend, it does not suggest that the legal category of the consumer has been erased from EU law. Core legislative instruments such as the UCPD, the UCTD, and the CRD continue to rely on the traditional notion of the consumer as a legally protected subject in B2C transactions. Case law from the Court of Justice of the European Union likewise reaffirms consumer-specific protections in various domains. However, the concern raised here is not one of formal elimination but of functional marginalization: as digital regulation increasingly adopts broader categories—such as “users,” “natural persons,” or “recipients”—the distinct vulnerabilities addressed by consumer law risk being obscured. The consumer remains a legal category, but its normative centrality is weakened in a regulatory environment that fragments protection across policy fields without a coherent, horizontal standard. This evolving imbalance justifies a call for renewed legal clarity and structural reinforcement of the consumer's role in digital governance.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Vanishing Consumer
paragraphs.text: The weakening of the consumer's regulatory visibility is not an isolated conceptual shift. It has practical consequences for the effectiveness of core consumer law instruments. The following Section turns to these doctrinal and functional limitations, focusing on the diminished capacity of the UCPD to address digital market realities.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: With the adoption of sweeping EU digital policy legislation, the limitations of traditional EU consumer law are becoming increasingly apparent, exposing a significant inadequacy

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: in addressing the challenges posed by the digital economy. Before the digital era transformed market dynamics, the most effective instrument in consumer law was the horizontal UCPD, widely regarded as a safety net against unfair market behaviour. The European Commission has long lauded the UCPD as a mechanism that upholds a high common level of consumer protection, ensuring that unfair commercial practices across all sectors are monitored by complementing and filling gaps in other EU laws (European Commission, 2021). A safety net is established by combining a prohibition of unfair commercial practices (Article 5(1) UCPD) with the underlying general clause based on the principle of professional diligence (Article 5(2) UCPD). This combination covers all B2C commercial practices “not caught by other provisions of the UCPD (i.e. that are neither misleading nor aggressive under Articles 6--9 or listed in Annex I)” (European Commission, 2021). By establishing a general unfairness standard, the UCPD continues to provide a flexible response to emerging forms of unfair commercial practices in traditional B2C transactions. However, flexibility alone is insufficient in a regulatory environment characterized by technological opacity, structural power imbalances, and real-time consumer manipulation. Yet this flexibility, once a strength, now reveals its limits in an environment where commercial unfairness is automated, personalized, and designed into user interfaces.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: The expectations that the UCPD would remain a sufficient tool for filling legal gaps were high. However, this same safety net now appears inadequate for the digital age. Research and regulatory experience highlight significant difficulties in applying the UCPD to digital contexts (Helberger et al. 2021a, b). The Directive was primarily designed to address power asymmetries in traditional B2C transactions. While such asymmetries persist and have arguably deepened, in today's digital economy, they manifest through complex, technology-driven dynamics. Digital platforms control market structures, curate the consumer experience, and manipulate the flow of information, thereby reshaping how power is exercised. This control fosters a digital market in which consumers are systematically disadvantaged—a structural vulnerability the UCPD was never intended to address. The UCPD remains grounded in the logic of counterbalancing power imbalances. However, its architecture presumes visible, traceable forms of unfairness in conventional B2C settings. Digital unfairness is built into algorithmic systems, embedded in interface design, and amplified by network effects—a structural shift the UCPD was never intended to address. Its effective application now depends on either heroic reinterpretation or structural reform.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: The new market phenomenon is best illustrated by the concepts of “digital asymmetry” and “digital vulnerability.” Digital asymmetry is defined as a situation of imbalance between traders and consumers concerning the knowledge and understanding of how a digital commercial practice functions (informational asymmetry), the imbalance in the commercial relationships that a digital environment creates and maintains (relational asymmetry), and structural differences in power that influence the autonomous decision-making process of the other party due to the control over data and/or a digital choice environment (structural asymmetry). Digital vulnerability refers to a universal state of susceptibility to exploiting differences in power within the trader-consumer relationship (Helberger et al. 2021a; Namysłowska, 2024a). Therefore, the average consumer benchmark—developed in the pre-digital era—is not conceptually viable. As digital environments systematically undermine consumer autonomy, the average consumer fiction cannot serve as the regulatory cornerstone (see also Esposito & Grochowski, 2022).

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: Furthermore, algorithmic bias and personalized exploitation in commercial practices lead to harms that are either underregulated or not captured in current legal provisions. As digital vulnerability intensifies alongside the exploitation of behavioural biases, the

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: traditional framework demonstrates its inability to protect consumers from sophisticated, multi-dimensional harm. The professional diligence standard under Article 5(2) UCPD, while originally designed to be a catch-all tool, struggles to address harms associated with algorithmic discrimination, personalized manipulation, or real-time interface design. As Helberger et al. (2021b) point out, these harms often fall beneath the visibility threshold of regulators and consumers alike.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: The digital age has broadened the scope of consumer harm, extending it beyond economic dimensions to encompass psychological, physical, and societal aspects. These harms include mental health issues, lack of sleep, and obsessive--compulsive symptoms, like compulsive buying among young adults; links to attention deficits and shorter attention spans, including risk factors for suicide and self-harm; information overload and excessive sensorial stimuli throughout the day; risks to neurodevelopment, learning, and memory; increased risk of early neurodegeneration; and not spending time being physically active, moving, being outside, shutting down, or relaxing (European Parliament, 2023). This reality renders traditional legal responses insufficient. Such harm resists simple economic valuation, yet it shapes the autonomy and well-being consumer law aims to protect.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: Another illustrative example of the inadequacy of the current legal framework is the interaction between the UCPD and EU digital policy legislation. Article 25 DSA introduces provisions aimed at curbing deceptive or manipulative online interfaces—tactics embedded in online interfaces that distort consumers' abilities to make free and informed decisions. However, these measures are limited to aspects of choice architecture and do not address addictive behavioural design. Moreover, their applicability is confined to providers of online platforms, leaving other services unregulated. Even more problematic is Article 25(2) DSA, which explicitly excludes the possibility of applying the prohibition in paragraph 1 to practices covered by the UCPD or the General Data Protection Regulation (“GDPR”). As clarified in Articles 2(4)(f) and (g), as well as in Recital 10, the DSA is intended to operate “without prejudice” to consumer and data protection law. While this aims to prevent normative overlap, it risks creating interpretive gaps where manipulative practices are not explicitly addressed by existing instruments (Raue, 2023).

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: The reliance on ex-post enforcement mechanisms also poses serious difficulties in the digital context. Digital commercial practices are often subtle, obscured by algorithmic decision-making and real-time adaptations, making detecting and quantifying consumer harm exceedingly difficult. Numerous studies confirm that consumers remain unaware of how interface architecture and algorithmic recommendations shape their choices.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: The European Commission found that 97% of leading websites contained at least one dark pattern, often unnoticed by users (European Commission, 2022). Empirical studies demonstrate that deceptive design patterns systematically reduce user awareness and erode autonomy, even among digital natives (e.g. Rossi et al. 2024). When harm is identified, however, proving that, for instance, algorithmic recommendations or personalized content led to a harmful decision requires technical expertise and access to data, which is a burden that disproportionately falls on consumers and often exceeds even the capabilities of national authorities.

section_path: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law and Its Resilient Revival: Reinventing Consumer Protection Against Unfair Digital Commercial Practices
Diagnosis: The Silent Death of EU Consumer Law
The Inadequacy of Traditional EU Consumer Frameworks in the Digital Age
paragraphs.text: Consequently, although a new legal issue does not automatically require a legislative overhaul, the intersection of rapid technological advancements and emerging market trends offers a strong justification for reassessing and, where necessary, reforming the legal frameworks (see Durovic, 2024). The ability of the UCPD to effectively safeguard consumers against unfair commercial practices has been fundamentally compromised by business practices associated with novel business models, technological design strategies, and unprecedented scale and scope. As such, targeted regulatory measures are crucial—a
